questions.promptTex: \text{What is the expression in terms of } y?
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,160 280,160 280,40' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='280' y2='40' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='264' y='144' width='16' height='16' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 254 160 A 26 26 0 0 0 256 142' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='246' y='154' font-size='14' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' font-style='italic'>θ</text><text x='160' y='178' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>14</text><text x='292' y='104' font-size='14' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='700' font-style='italic'>y</text></svg>
hints: From angle $\theta$, the side $14$ is **adjacent** and $y$ is **opposite**.
Two legs (opp + adj) → use **tangent**: $\tan\theta = \dfrac{y}{14}$.
Solve for $y$.
questions.explanation: From $\theta$: adjacent $= 14$, opposite $= y$. So $\tan\theta = \dfrac{y}{14}$, giving $y = 14\tan\theta$.
steps: **Label the sides relative to $\theta$.** $\theta$ sits at the right-hand vertex. The side **across** from $\theta$ is the vertical leg, so opposite $= y$. The side **next to** $\theta$ (not the hypotenuse) is the horizontal leg, so adjacent $= 14$.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='40,160 280,160 280,40' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='280' y2='40' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='264' y='144' width='16' height='16' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 254 160 A 26 26 0 0 0 256 142' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='246' y='154' font-size='14' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' font-style='italic'>θ</text><text x='160' y='178' font-size='13' fill='#10b981' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>adjacent = 14</text><text x='292' y='100' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='600'>opp = y</text><text x='130' y='98' font-size='12' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='600' transform='rotate(-26 130 98)'>hypotenuse</text></svg>

steps: **Pick the right ratio.** Both legs involved, no hypotenuse → **tangent**.
Set up: $$\tan\theta = \dfrac{\text{opp}}{\text{adj}} = \dfrac{y}{14}$$
Multiply both sides by $14$: $$y = 14\tan\theta$$

questions.promptTex: \text{If } \sin B = \tfrac{3}{5} \text{ and } \tan B < 0, \text{ then } \cos B = ?
hints: $\sin B > 0$ means $B$ is in Quadrant I or II.
$\tan B < 0$ means $B$ is in Quadrant II or IV.
Both conditions → Quadrant II, where cosine is **negative**.
questions.explanation: $\sin B = 3/5 > 0$ and $\tan B < 0$ put $B$ in Quadrant II. Using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$: $\cos^2 B = 1 - \tfrac{9}{25} = \tfrac{16}{25}$, so $\cos B = \pm\tfrac{4}{5}$. In Q2, cosine is negative, so $\cos B = -\tfrac{4}{5}$.
steps: **Identify the quadrant.** Use a sign chart (ASTC). $\sin B > 0$ → Q1 or Q2. $\tan B < 0$ → Q2 or Q4. The overlap is **Q2**.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='240' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='130' y1='10' x2='130' y2='210' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='22' y='12' width='106' height='96' fill='#fef3c7' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='132' y='12' width='106' height='96' fill='#dcfce7' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='22' y='112' width='106' height='96' fill='#fef3c7' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='132' y='112' width='106' height='96' fill='#fef3c7' opacity='0.7'/><text x='75' y='30' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>Q2</text><text x='75' y='48' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>sin +</text><text x='75' y='62' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#dc2626'>cos −  tan −</text><text x='185' y='30' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>Q1</text><text x='185' y='48' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>all +</text><text x='75' y='148' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>Q3</text><text x='75' y='166' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>tan +</text><text x='185' y='148' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>Q4</text><text x='185' y='166' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>cos +</text></svg>

steps: **Use the Pythagorean identity** to find $\cos B$: $$\sin^2 B + \cos^2 B = 1$$ $$\left(\tfrac{3}{5}\right)^2 + \cos^2 B = 1$$
Solve: $$\cos^2 B = 1 - \tfrac{9}{25} = \tfrac{16}{25}$$ $$\cos B = \pm\tfrac{4}{5}$$
**Pick the right sign.** In Quadrant II, cosine is negative, so $$\cos B = -\tfrac{4}{5}$$

questions.promptTex: \sec(x) \cdot \cot(x) = ?
hints: Rewrite each in terms of $\sin$ and $\cos$.
$\sec x = \dfrac{1}{\cos x}$ and $\cot x = \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$.
questions.explanation: $\sec x \cdot \cot x = \dfrac{1}{\cos x} \cdot \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x} = \dfrac{1}{\sin x} = \csc x$.
steps: **Convert to sine and cosine** — the safest first move with any identity. $$\sec x = \dfrac{1}{\cos x}, \quad \cot x = \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$$
Multiply: $$\sec x \cdot \cot x = \dfrac{1}{\cos x} \cdot \dfrac{\cos x}{\sin x}$$
The $\cos x$ cancels: $$= \dfrac{1}{\sin x}$$
And $\dfrac{1}{\sin x} = \csc x$, so the answer is $\csc x$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Standing in a lighthouse, } 150 \text{ feet above the shore, I spot a boat at} \\ \text{an angle of depression of } 11°. \text{ How far away is the boat from shore?}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='160' width='420' height='60' fill='#dbeafe'/><line x1='0' y1='160' x2='420' y2='160' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='40' y='50' width='28' height='110' fill='#f8fafc' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='32' y='40' width='44' height='14' fill='#dc2626'/><circle cx='54' cy='34' r='4' fill='#fde68a'/><path d='M 360 162 L 400 162 L 392 174 L 368 174 Z' fill='#92400e' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='380' y1='162' x2='380' y2='140' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='380,140 396,158 380,158' fill='#fef3c7' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='54' y1='52' x2='54' y2='160' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><line x1='54' y1='160' x2='380' y2='160' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><line x1='54' y1='52' x2='380' y2='160' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M 54 78 A 26 26 0 0 0 76 86' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='62' y='80' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>11°</text><text x='34' y='112' font-size='13' text-anchor='end'>150 ft</text><text x='216' y='180' font-size='13' text-anchor='middle'>d = ?</text></svg>
hints: Sketch a right triangle: the lighthouse is vertical (height $150$), the line of sight is the hypotenuse, the horizontal distance $d$ is the bottom leg.
The angle of depression at the top equals the angle of elevation at the boat (alternate interior angles).
From the $11°$ angle at the boat: opposite $= 150$, adjacent $= d$. Use $\tan 11° = \dfrac{150}{d}$.
questions.explanation: Use $\tan 11° = \dfrac{150}{d}$ → $d = \dfrac{150}{\tan 11°} \approx \dfrac{150}{0.1944} \approx 771.7$ feet, which rounds to $772$.
steps: **Sketch the situation.** The lighthouse is a vertical leg of $150$ ft. The boat is on the water some distance $d$ away. The line of sight from the top of the lighthouse to the boat is the hypotenuse.

**Where is the $11°$ angle?** The *angle of depression* is measured from the horizontal at the observer (the top of the lighthouse) **down** to the line of sight. By alternate interior angles, the angle of elevation from the boat up to the observer is also $11°$. Working from the boat's vertex makes the triangle easier to read.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 420 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><rect x='0' y='160' width='420' height='60' fill='#dbeafe'/><line x1='0' y1='160' x2='420' y2='160' stroke='#3b82f6' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='40' y='50' width='28' height='110' fill='#f8fafc' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='32' y='40' width='44' height='14' fill='#dc2626'/><line x1='54' y1='52' x2='54' y2='160' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='54' y1='160' x2='380' y2='160' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='54' y1='52' x2='380' y2='160' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='54' y='148' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 354 160 A 28 28 0 0 0 351 148' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='340' y='154' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>11°</text><text x='28' y='112' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='600' text-anchor='end'>opp = 150</text><text x='216' y='180' font-size='13' fill='#10b981' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='600'>adj = d</text></svg>

steps: **Choose the ratio.** From the boat's $11°$ angle: opposite $= 150$, adjacent $= d$. Both legs in play → **tangent**.
Set up: $$\tan 11° = \dfrac{150}{d}$$
Solve for $d$ (it's in the denominator, so multiply across, then divide): $$d = \dfrac{150}{\tan 11°} \approx \dfrac{150}{0.1944} \approx 771.7$$
Rounded: $d \approx 772$ feet.
**Quick sanity check.** Small angle of depression $\Rightarrow$ the boat is far away. $11°$ is small, so $d$ should be much bigger than $150$ — and $772 \gg 150$. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{Use the subtraction identity } \sin(A - B) = \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B. \\ \text{Since } \dfrac{\pi}{4} - \dfrac{\pi}{6} = \dfrac{\pi}{12}, \text{ what is } \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{12}\right)?
hints: Apply the formula with $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ and $B = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
$\sin\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \cos\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, $\sin\tfrac{\pi}{6} = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $\cos\tfrac{\pi}{6} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.
Combine the terms over a common denominator of $4$.
questions.explanation: $\sin\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4} - \tfrac{\pi}{6}\right) = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2} = \tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4} = \tfrac{\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4}$.
steps: **Pick $A$ and $B$.** Let $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ (which is $45°$) and $B = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ (which is $30°$). Then $A - B = \dfrac{\pi}{12}$ (which is $15°$).

**Recall the special values:** $$\sin\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \quad \cos\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$$ $$\sin\tfrac{\pi}{6} = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad \cos\tfrac{\pi}{6} = \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$
**Plug into the identity** $\sin(A-B) = \sin A\cos B - \cos A\sin B$: $$\sin\tfrac{\pi}{12} = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}$$
Multiply the fractions: $$= \tfrac{\sqrt{6}}{4} - \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{4}$$
Combine over the common denominator: $$\sin\tfrac{\pi}{12} = \dfrac{\sqrt{6} - \sqrt{2}}{4}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Which equation best describes the periodic function shown?}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='160' x2='440' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='40' x2='440' y2='40' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='2 2'/><line x1='20' y1='100' x2='440' y2='100' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='2 2'/><line x1='100' y1='30' x2='100' y2='200' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='200' y1='150' x2='200' y2='170' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='300' y1='150' x2='300' y2='170' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='400' y1='150' x2='400' y2='170' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><text x='200' y='184' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>2π</text><text x='300' y='184' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>4π</text><text x='400' y='184' font-size='12' text-anchor='middle'>6π</text><text x='86' y='44' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>10</text><text x='86' y='104' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>5</text><text x='86' y='164' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>0</text><path d='M 100 124 Q 150 28 200 124 Q 250 220 300 124 Q 350 28 400 124' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.2'/></svg>
hints: **Period** = distance from one peak to the next. Reading off the graph, the peaks are $4\pi$ apart.
For $\sin(Bx)$, period $= \dfrac{2\pi}{B}$. If the period is $4\pi$, then $B = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
**Midline** is the horizontal centre of the wave $\approx 3$, **amplitude** = (max − min)/2 = (7−(−1))/2 = 4.
questions.explanation: Period $= 4\pi$ gives $B = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Midline at $y = 3$ gives the +3 shift. Amplitude $4$ gives the coefficient. The equation is $4\sin(\tfrac{1}{2}x) + 3$.
steps: **Find the period.** Look at peak-to-peak distance on the graph: the wave peaks twice, $4\pi$ apart. So **period $= 4\pi$**.
**Find $B$.** For $y = a\sin(Bx) + k$, period $= \dfrac{2\pi}{B}$. Solve: $$\dfrac{2\pi}{B} = 4\pi \;\Rightarrow\; B = \dfrac{1}{2}$$
**Find the midline $k$.** The wave oscillates between roughly $y = -1$ and $y = 7$. The midline is the average: $$k = \dfrac{7 + (-1)}{2} = 3$$
**Find the amplitude $a$.** Amplitude is the distance from midline to peak: $$a = 7 - 3 = 4$$
**Assemble:** $$y = 4\sin\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}x\right) + 3$$

questions.promptTex: \text{If placed on the unit circle, which angle measure differs from the others?}
hints: Two angles are **coterminal** when they differ by a multiple of $360°$ (or $2\pi$).
Reduce each to a single rotation by adding or subtracting $360°$.
Convert radian measures: $-\tfrac{5\pi}{6} = -150°$ and $\tfrac{19\pi}{6} = 570°$.
questions.explanation: All others reduce to $210°$. Only $390°$ reduces to $30°$, putting it in Quadrant I instead of Quadrant III.
steps: **Reduce each angle to its standard $[0°, 360°)$ form** by adding or subtracting $360°$ until it lands in that range. Two angles are coterminal iff they reduce to the same value.
Four of the five are $210°$ (Quadrant III). Only **(d) $390°$** lands at $30°$ in Quadrant I — that's the odd one out.

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle with } AB = 4,\ BC = 5,\ AC = 7. \\ \text{Using } c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C, \\ \text{what is the measure of angle } C \text{ (in degrees)?}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='80,180 240,180 130,40' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='130' cy='40' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='80' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='240' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='130' y='30' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>A</text><text x='66' y='192' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>B</text><text x='254' y='192' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>C</text><text x='160' y='194' font-size='13' fill='#4f46e5' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>BC = 5 (a)</text><text x='98' y='110' font-size='13' fill='#10b981' text-anchor='end' font-weight='700'>AB = 4 (c)</text><text x='200' y='110' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' text-anchor='start' font-weight='700'>AC = 7 (b)</text></svg>
hints: $C$ is the angle opposite side $c$ (the convention in the formula). Side $c$ is **across** from $C$, which is $AB = 4$.
Identify $a$ (opposite $A$) $= BC = 5$, $b$ (opposite $B$) $= AC = 7$, $c$ (opposite $C$) $= AB = 4$.
Plug into $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$ and solve for $\cos C$.
questions.explanation: $16 = 25 + 49 - 70\cos C \Rightarrow 70\cos C = 58 \Rightarrow \cos C = \tfrac{58}{70} \approx 0.829 \Rightarrow C \approx 34°$.
steps: **Match sides to letters.** The convention is: side $a$ is opposite angle $A$, side $b$ is opposite $B$, side $c$ is opposite $C$. Reading off the triangle: $$a = BC = 5,\quad b = AC = 7,\quad c = AB = 4$$
**Plug into the Law of Cosines.** $$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos C$$ $$4^2 = 5^2 + 7^2 - 2(5)(7)\cos C$$
Isolate $\cos C$: $$70\cos C = 74 - 16 = 58$$ $$\cos C = \tfrac{58}{70} \approx 0.8286$$
Take inverse cosine: $$C = \cos^{-1}(0.8286) \approx 34.05°$$
Rounded: $C \approx 34°$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Angle } B \text{ is in standard position. Its terminal side passes through } (-3, 7). \\ \cot(B) = ?
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='160' x2='300' y2='160' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='160' y1='20' x2='160' y2='220' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.5'/><polygon points='300,160 290,156 290,164' fill='#94a3b8'/><polygon points='160,20 156,30 164,30' fill='#94a3b8'/><text x='306' y='164' font-size='12' fill='#64748b'>x</text><text x='164' y='18' font-size='12' fill='#64748b'>y</text><line x1='160' y1='160' x2='100' y2='20' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2.5'/><polygon points='100,20 112,24 106,34' fill='#4f46e5'/><circle cx='100' cy='20' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><path d='M 200 160 A 40 40 0 1 0 142 24' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='180' y='110' font-size='14' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' font-style='italic'>B</text><text x='84' y='14' font-size='12' fill='#1e293b' font-weight='600'>(−3, 7)</text></svg>
hints: For a point $(x, y)$ on the terminal side: $\tan B = \dfrac{y}{x}$ and $\cot B = \dfrac{x}{y}$.
Here $x = -3$ and $y = 7$.
$\cot B = \dfrac{x}{y} = \dfrac{-3}{7}$.
questions.explanation: For a point $(x, y)$ on the terminal side, $\cot B = \dfrac{x}{y} = \dfrac{-3}{7}$.
steps: **Setup.** For an angle $B$ in standard position with a point $(x, y)$ on its terminal side, the trig ratios use the distance $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$: $$\sin B = \tfrac{y}{r},\ \cos B = \tfrac{x}{r},\ \tan B = \tfrac{y}{x}$$
**Cotangent is the reciprocal of tangent:** $$\cot B = \dfrac{1}{\tan B} = \dfrac{x}{y}$$
**Sanity check the sign.** The point $(-3, 7)$ is in Quadrant II, where cosine is negative and sine is positive — so tangent (and cotangent) must be negative. $-\tfrac{3}{7}$ is negative ✓.

questions.promptTex: \text{If } \tan(x) = 1, \text{ and } x \text{ is between } \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ and } \dfrac{3\pi}{2}, \\ \text{then what is the value of } \dfrac{x}{3}?
hints: Where is $\tan = 1$? Most basic answer: $x = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ — but that's in Quadrant I, *not* in the given interval.
Tangent has period $\pi$, so $\tan = 1$ also at $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \pi = \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$. That's in Quadrant III, which fits the interval $\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}, \dfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)$.
Once you have $x = \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$, divide by $3$.
questions.explanation: $\tan x = 1$ at $x = \tfrac{\pi}{4} + n\pi$. In $\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)$, that's $x = \tfrac{5\pi}{4}$. So $\tfrac{x}{3} = \tfrac{5\pi}{12}$.
steps: **Find all $x$ where $\tan x = 1$.** The principal value is $x = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$. Since tangent has period $\pi$, the general solution is $$x = \tfrac{\pi}{4} + n\pi$$

**Pick the value in the given range** $\left(\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{3\pi}{2}\right)$. Trying values of $n$: $n=0$ gives $\tfrac{\pi}{4}$ (too small). $n=1$ gives $\tfrac{\pi}{4} + \pi = \tfrac{5\pi}{4}$. Check: $\tfrac{\pi}{2} < \tfrac{5\pi}{4} < \tfrac{3\pi}{2}$ ✓
**Why does $\tan = 1$ here?** In Quadrant III both $\sin$ and $\cos$ are negative, so the ratio $\tan = \tfrac{\sin}{\cos}$ is positive. The reference angle is $\tfrac{\pi}{4}$, so $\tan = 1$.
**Divide by 3:** $$\dfrac{x}{3} = \dfrac{5\pi/4}{3} = \dfrac{5\pi}{12}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Which graph best represents } y = -\cos(x) + 5?
choices: a) wave centred above the x-axis, **starting at a peak** at $x = 0$
b) wave centred above the x-axis, **starting at a trough** at $x = 0$
d) tall-amplitude wave that crosses the x-axis (range from negative to positive)
e) tall-amplitude wave starting at a peak
hints: The $+5$ shifts the whole graph up by $5$, so the **midline** is $y = 5$ — entirely above the x-axis.
Amplitude is $1$, so $y$ ranges from $4$ to $6$ — a *small* wave, not a tall one.
At $x = 0$: $y = -\cos(0) + 5 = -1 + 5 = 4$ — that's the **minimum**, so the wave starts at a trough.
questions.explanation: The midline is $y = 5$ (small wave, well above the x-axis). The negative sign flips the cosine, so at $x = 0$ the graph is at its **minimum** ($y = 4$) — i.e., it starts at a trough.
steps: **Read off the transformations of $y = \cos x$.**
**$+5$** → vertical shift up by $5$. The midline is $y = 5$.
**$-$ in front** → reflection across the midline. So instead of starting at a peak at $x = 0$, the graph starts at a trough.
**No coefficient on $\cos$** → amplitude is $1$. The wave ranges from $5 - 1 = 4$ to $5 + 1 = 6$.
**Plug in $x = 0$ to double-check:** $$y = -\cos(0) + 5 = -1 + 5 = 4$$ That's the minimum value. So the graph starts at a trough and rises.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='180' x2='440' y2='180' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='80' y1='10' x2='80' y2='210' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='440' y2='80' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='72' y='84' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>y=5</text><text x='72' y='184' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>0</text><path d='M 80 100 Q 130 60 180 80 Q 230 100 280 80 Q 330 60 380 80 Q 405 90 430 80' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.2'/><circle cx='80' cy='100' r='4' fill='#dc2626'/><text x='90' y='112' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626'>start at trough (y=4)</text></svg>

steps: Matches choice **(b)**: a small wave with midline above the x-axis, starting at a trough.

questions.promptTex: \text{In the system } \begin{cases} y = 3 \\ y = 4\sin(x) \end{cases}, \\ \text{how many intersections does the graph have?}
hints: Setting the two equal: $4\sin x = 3 \Rightarrow \sin x = \tfrac{3}{4}$. Is this value attainable?
Since $|\tfrac{3}{4}| \le 1$, yes — and it happens infinitely often because $\sin$ is periodic.
In each period of $2\pi$, $\sin x = \tfrac{3}{4}$ has **two** solutions. Across all real $x$, that's infinitely many.
questions.explanation: $4\sin x = 3$ gives $\sin x = 0.75$, which has two solutions per period of $2\pi$. With no interval restriction, the sine wave keeps crossing the horizontal line $y = 3$ forever, so there are infinitely many intersections.
steps: **Set the equations equal:** $$4\sin x = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; \sin x = \tfrac{3}{4}$$
**Check that solutions exist.** Sine values live in $[-1, 1]$, and $\tfrac{3}{4}$ is inside that range, so there ARE intersections.
**How many per period?** In one period $[0, 2\pi]$, $\sin x = \tfrac{3}{4}$ has exactly **two** solutions: one in Q1 (going up) and one in Q2 (coming back down).

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 460 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='440' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='80' y1='10' x2='80' y2='210' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='20' y1='62' x2='440' y2='62' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><text x='446' y='66' font-size='12' fill='#16a34a' font-weight='600'>y = 3</text><path d='M 80 110 Q 110 -10 140 110 Q 170 230 200 110 Q 230 -10 260 110 Q 290 230 320 110 Q 350 -10 380 110 Q 410 230 440 110' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2'/><text x='446' y='208' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600'>y = 4 sin x</text><circle cx='105' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='145' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='225' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='265' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='345' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='385' cy='62' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/></svg>

steps: **Across all of $\mathbb{R}$**, sine repeats forever, so it crosses $y = 3$ infinitely often. That's **more than 3** intersections.

hints: Reduce $450°$ to an angle in $[0°, 360°)$ by subtracting full rotations of $360°$.
steps: **Reduce to a standard angle.** Cosine has period $360°$, so subtract $360°$: $$450° - 360° = 90°$$ Cosine of $450°$ equals cosine of $90°$.
**Read off the unit-circle value.** At $90°$ the point on the unit circle is $(0, 1)$. Cosine is the *x*-coordinate, so $$\cos 90° = 0$$
Answer: $\cos 450° = 0$.

questions.promptTex: \text{The period of the function } y = 5\sin(4\theta + 60°) \text{ is:}
hints: Period of $y = a\sin(B\theta + C)$ is $\dfrac{360°}{|B|}$ (degrees) or $\dfrac{2\pi}{|B|}$ (radians).
The phase shift ($+60°$) and amplitude ($5$) don't affect the period.
With $B = 4$: period $= \dfrac{360°}{4} = 90°$.
questions.explanation: For $y = a\sin(B\theta + C)$, period $= \dfrac{360°}{|B|}$. With $B = 4$, period $= 90°$. (Amplitude $5$ and phase shift $60°$ don't change the period.)
steps: **Recall the period formula.** For $y = a\sin(B\theta + C) + D$ in degrees, $$\text{period} = \dfrac{360°}{|B|}$$ The horizontal stretch/compression depends *only* on $B$. The amplitude $a$, phase shift $C$, and vertical shift $D$ don't affect period.
**Identify $B$** from $y = 5\sin(4\theta + 60°)$: $B = 4$.
**Plug in:** $$\text{period} = \dfrac{360°}{4} = 90°$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A loading dock ramp must rise at an angle of } 20° \text{ from the ground.} \\ \text{If it must reach a height of } 8 \text{ feet, what is the minimum length} \\ \text{of the ramp? (Use } \sin 20° \approx 0.342 \text{)}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='0' y1='160' x2='360' y2='160' stroke='#92400e' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='240' y='70' width='90' height='90' fill='#fde68a' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='240' y='62' width='90' height='12' fill='#92400e'/><polygon points='40,160 240,160 240,70' fill='#dbeafe' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='40' y1='160' x2='240' y2='70' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='240' y1='70' x2='240' y2='160' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='2'/><rect x='228' y='148' width='12' height='12' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 70 160 A 30 30 0 0 0 68 152' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='76' y='154' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>20°</text><text x='140' y='108' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600' transform='rotate(-24 140 108)'>L = ?</text><text x='252' y='118' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='600'>8 ft</text></svg>
hints: Sketch: the ramp is the **hypotenuse**, the height ($8$) is the side **opposite** the $20°$ angle.
opp + hyp + angle → use **sine**: $\sin 20° = \dfrac{8}{L}$.
Solve for $L$ (it's in the denominator): $L = \dfrac{8}{\sin 20°}$.
questions.explanation: $L = \dfrac{8}{\sin 20°} \approx \dfrac{8}{0.342} \approx 23.4$ ft, which rounds up to $24$ ft.
steps: **Sketch the right triangle.** The ramp slopes up from the ground; the height $8$ is vertical. The $20°$ angle is between the ramp and the ground.
**Identify the sides relative to the $20°$ angle.** Hypotenuse $= L$ (the ramp itself, the slanted side). Opposite $= 8$ (the height).
**Pick the ratio.** Opposite + hypotenuse → **sine**: $$\sin 20° = \dfrac{8}{L}$$
Multiply both sides by $L$, then divide by $\sin 20°$: $$L = \dfrac{8}{\sin 20°}$$
Plug in $\sin 20° \approx 0.342$: $$L \approx \dfrac{8}{0.342} \approx 23.4 \text{ ft}$$
Since the ramp must *reach* $8$ ft, we round **up** to the nearest answer choice: $L \approx 24$ ft.

questions.promptTex: \text{Simplify } (1 + \sin R)(1 - \sin R)
hints: Recognize the **difference of squares** pattern: $(1 + a)(1 - a) = 1 - a^2$.
With $a = \sin R$: $(1 + \sin R)(1 - \sin R) = 1 - \sin^2 R$.
From the Pythagorean identity, $1 - \sin^2 R = \cos^2 R$.
questions.explanation: $(1 + \sin R)(1 - \sin R) = 1 - \sin^2 R = \cos^2 R$ (Pythagorean identity).
steps: **Spot the pattern** — this is a difference of squares: $$(a + b)(a - b) = a^2 - b^2$$ with $a = 1$ and $b = \sin R$.
Multiply: $$(1 + \sin R)(1 - \sin R) = 1^2 - \sin^2 R = 1 - \sin^2 R$$
**Apply the Pythagorean identity** $\sin^2 R + \cos^2 R = 1$, rearranged to $1 - \sin^2 R = \cos^2 R$: $$1 - \sin^2 R = \cos^2 R$$

questions.promptTex: \text{Triangle } JHK \text{ has } JH = 10, \text{ angle } J = 75°, \text{ angle } K = 40°. \\ \text{Find the length of } JK. \\ \text{(Hint: Law of Sines: } \dfrac{a}{\sin A} = \dfrac{b}{\sin B} = \dfrac{c}{\sin C}\text{)}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='200,30 70,180 290,180' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><circle cx='200' cy='30' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='70' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><circle cx='290' cy='180' r='4' fill='#1e293b'/><text x='200' y='22' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>J</text><text x='58' y='192' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>H</text><text x='304' y='192' font-size='14' text-anchor='middle' font-weight='700'>K</text><path d='M 212 50 A 24 24 0 0 0 188 50' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='200' y='62' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>75°</text><path d='M 268 174 A 26 26 0 0 0 282 158' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='268' y='162' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>40°</text><text x='118' y='102' font-size='13' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='700'>10</text><text x='254' y='102' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='700'>JK = ?</text></svg>
hints: Find angle $H$ first: $180° - 75° - 40° = 65°$.
Side $JH$ (length $10$) is **opposite** angle $K = 40°$. Side $JK$ is **opposite** angle $H = 65°$.
Set up: $\dfrac{JK}{\sin 65°} = \dfrac{10}{\sin 40°}$, then solve.
questions.explanation: $JK = \dfrac{10 \sin 65°}{\sin 40°} \approx \dfrac{10 \cdot 0.906}{0.643} \approx 14.1$.
steps: **Find the third angle.** Angles in a triangle sum to $180°$: $$\angle H = 180° - 75° - 40° = 65°$$

**Match sides to opposite angles.** In a triangle, each side is opposite the angle that does *not* touch it. Side $JH$ does not touch vertex $K$, so $JH$ is opposite $\angle K$. Similarly, $JK$ is opposite $\angle H$. $$JH = 10 \text{ is opposite } \angle K = 40°$$ $$JK = ? \text{ is opposite } \angle H = 65°$$
**Apply the Law of Sines:** $$\dfrac{JK}{\sin H} = \dfrac{JH}{\sin K}$$ $$\dfrac{JK}{\sin 65°} = \dfrac{10}{\sin 40°}$$
Solve for $JK$: $$JK = \dfrac{10 \sin 65°}{\sin 40°}$$
Plug in $\sin 65° \approx 0.906$ and $\sin 40° \approx 0.643$: $$JK \approx \dfrac{10 \cdot 0.906}{0.643} \approx \dfrac{9.06}{0.643} \approx 14.1$$
**Sanity check.** $\angle H = 65°$ is larger than $\angle K = 40°$, so the side opposite $H$ should be longer than the side opposite $K$. Indeed $14.1 > 10$ ✓.

questions.promptTex: \text{On the unit circle, the point } \left(x,\ \tfrac{3}{5}\right) \text{ lies in Quadrant II.} \\ x = ?
hints: Every point $(x, y)$ on the unit circle satisfies $x^2 + y^2 = 1$.
$x = \pm\tfrac{4}{5}$. In Quadrant II, $x$ is negative — pick the negative root.
steps: **Use the unit-circle equation.** Every point $(x, y)$ on the unit circle satisfies $$x^2 + y^2 = 1$$
**Plug in the known $y$.** With $y = \tfrac{3}{5}$: $$x^2 + \left(\tfrac{3}{5}\right)^2 = 1$$ $$x^2 + \tfrac{9}{25} = 1$$
Take square roots: $$x = \pm \tfrac{4}{5}$$

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='240' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='130' y1='20' x2='130' y2='220' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='130' cy='120' r='90' fill='none' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='1.6'/><text x='75' y='40' font-size='13' fill='#16a34a' font-weight='600'>Q2: x < 0</text><text x='160' y='40' font-size='12' fill='#94a3b8'>Q1</text><circle cx='58' cy='66' r='5' fill='#dc2626'/><line x1='130' y1='120' x2='58' y2='66' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='46' y='58' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>(−4/5, 3/5)</text></svg>

steps: **Pick the right sign for Quadrant II.** In Q2, $x < 0$ and $y > 0$. So $$x = -\tfrac{4}{5}$$

questions.promptTex: \text{A bicycle wheel makes } 10 \text{ rotations per minute.} \\ \text{If a piece of gum is stuck on the wheel,} \\ \text{how many radians does the gum travel per minute?}
hints: One full rotation around a circle covers $2\pi$ radians.
Total angle (in radians) = (number of rotations) $\times 2\pi$.
$10 \times 2\pi = 20\pi \approx 62.83$, which rounds to $63$.
questions.explanation: One rotation $= 2\pi$ radians. So $10$ rotations $= 20\pi \approx 62.83 \approx 63$ radians per minute.
steps: **Recall the conversion.** One full rotation around a circle equals $2\pi$ radians (about $6.283$).
**Multiply.** Each minute the gum makes $10$ rotations, so the angle swept is $$10 \text{ rotations} \times 2\pi\ \tfrac{\text{rad}}{\text{rotation}} = 20\pi \text{ rad/min}$$
**Convert to a decimal:** $$20\pi \approx 20 \times 3.14159 \approx 62.83$$
Rounded to the nearest whole number: $63$ radians per minute.
**Why not $20$ (choice b)?** That answer forgets the $\pi$ factor. Radians-per-rotation is $2\pi$, not $2$. Always include $\pi$ when converting rotations to radians.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the area of triangle } ABC?
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='60,200 260,200 60,40' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='260' y2='200' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2.5'/><rect x='60' y='188' width='14' height='14' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 232 200 A 28 28 0 0 0 220 178' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='220' y='190' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>50°</text><text x='180' y='110' font-size='14' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='700'>12</text></svg>
hints: The side labeled $12$ is the **hypotenuse**. Use the $50°$ angle to split it into the two legs.
Vertical leg (opposite $50°$) $= 12 \sin 50°$. Horizontal leg (adjacent to $50°$) $= 12 \cos 50°$.
Area of a right triangle $= \tfrac{1}{2}(\text{leg}_1)(\text{leg}_2)$.
questions.explanation: Legs: $12 \sin 50° \approx 9.19$ and $12 \cos 50° \approx 7.71$. Area $= \tfrac{1}{2}(9.19)(7.71) \approx 35.4$.
steps: **Identify the sides.** The right angle is at the bottom-left, $50°$ at the bottom-right, and the hypotenuse $= 12$ is the slanted side connecting them.
**Find the two legs using the $50°$ angle.** From the $50°$ angle, the vertical side is opposite and the horizontal side is adjacent.
Vertical leg (opposite): $$12 \sin 50° \approx 12 \times 0.766 \approx 9.19$$
Horizontal leg (adjacent): $$12 \cos 50° \approx 12 \times 0.643 \approx 7.71$$

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 320 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><polygon points='60,200 260,200 60,40' fill='#eef2ff' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='260' y2='200' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2.5'/><line x1='60' y1='40' x2='60' y2='200' stroke='#7c3aed' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='60' y1='200' x2='260' y2='200' stroke='#10b981' stroke-width='3'/><rect x='60' y='188' width='14' height='14' fill='none' stroke='#1e293b' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 232 200 A 28 28 0 0 0 220 178' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.5'/><text x='220' y='190' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>50°</text><text x='180' y='110' font-size='13' fill='#4f46e5' font-weight='700'>hyp = 12</text><text x='50' y='124' font-size='13' fill='#7c3aed' font-weight='700' text-anchor='end'>9.19</text><text x='160' y='222' font-size='13' fill='#10b981' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>7.71</text></svg>

steps: **Area formula** for a right triangle uses the two legs as base and height: $$A = \tfrac{1}{2}(\text{leg}_1)(\text{leg}_2)$$

**Shortcut you can remember:** for a right triangle with hypotenuse $h$ and acute angle $\theta$, $$A = \tfrac{1}{2} h^2 \sin\theta \cos\theta = \tfrac{1}{4} h^2 \sin(2\theta)$$ Here that gives $\tfrac{1}{4}(144)\sin 100° \approx 36 \times 0.985 \approx 35.4$ — same answer.

questions.promptTex: \text{An angle in standard position has a measure equivalent to } \dfrac{17\pi}{4} \text{ radians.} \\ \text{If drawn on the coordinate plane, the terminal side lands in which quadrant?}
hints: Reduce $\dfrac{17\pi}{4}$ modulo $2\pi$ to find its coterminal angle in $[0, 2\pi)$.
Subtract $2\pi = \tfrac{8\pi}{4}$ as many times as needed.
$\tfrac{17\pi}{4} - 2 \cdot 2\pi = \tfrac{17\pi}{4} - \tfrac{16\pi}{4} = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$. Which quadrant is $\pi/4$ in?
questions.explanation: $\dfrac{17\pi}{4} - 4\pi = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$, which is $45°$ — in Quadrant I.
steps: **Reduce modulo $2\pi$.** $2\pi = \dfrac{8\pi}{4}$. Subtract multiples until the angle lands in $[0, 2\pi)$.
First subtraction: $$\dfrac{17\pi}{4} - \dfrac{8\pi}{4} = \dfrac{9\pi}{4}$$
Still larger than $2\pi$. Second subtraction: $$\dfrac{9\pi}{4} - \dfrac{8\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$$
**Locate $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.** That's $45°$, which lies between $0$ and $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ — squarely in **Quadrant I**.

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 260 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='120' x2='240' y2='120' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='130' y1='20' x2='130' y2='220' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='130' cy='120' r='90' fill='none' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='130' y1='120' x2='194' y2='56' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.5'/><polygon points='194,56 184,58 188,70' fill='#dc2626'/><path d='M 170 120 A 40 40 0 0 0 154 92' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='168' y='104' font-size='12' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>π/4</text><text x='180' y='40' font-size='13' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>Q I</text><text x='100' y='240' font-size='11' fill='#64748b' text-anchor='middle'>17π/4 → π/4 after subtracting 4π</text></svg>

steps: **Shortcut:** $\dfrac{17\pi}{4} = 4\pi + \dfrac{\pi}{4}$. Two full rotations ($4\pi$) bring you back to the starting axis, leaving just $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ — Quadrant I.

questions.promptTex: \text{The equation } 2\sin^2 x + 3\sin x + 1 = 0,\ \text{ where } 0° < x < 360°, \\ \text{has } 3 \text{ solutions. The } \textbf{sum} \text{ of those solutions is:}
hints: Let $u = \sin x$. The equation becomes the quadratic $2u^2 + 3u + 1 = 0$.
Factor: $(2u + 1)(u + 1) = 0$, so $\sin x = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ or $\sin x = -1$.
$\sin x = -\tfrac{1}{2}$ has solutions $x = 210°$ and $x = 330°$. $\sin x = -1$ gives $x = 270°$.
steps: **Substitute** $u = \sin x$ to turn the trig equation into a quadratic: $$2u^2 + 3u + 1 = 0$$
**Factor the quadratic.** Look for two numbers that multiply to $2 \cdot 1 = 2$ and add to $3$ → $2$ and $1$: $$(2u + 1)(u + 1) = 0$$
**Solve each factor:** $$2u + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow u = -\tfrac{1}{2}$$ $$u + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow u = -1$$
**Convert back to $x$.** Sine is negative in Q3 and Q4.
**Case 1: $\sin x = -\tfrac{1}{2}$.** Reference angle $30°$. Solutions in $(0°, 360°)$: $$x = 180° + 30° = 210° \quad (\text{Q3})$$ $$x = 360° - 30° = 330° \quad (\text{Q4})$$
**Case 2: $\sin x = -1$.** Sine reaches $-1$ only at $$x = 270°$$

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='460' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='40' y1='20' x2='40' y2='200' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='20' y1='150' x2='460' y2='150' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><line x1='20' y1='190' x2='460' y2='190' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><path d='M 40 110 Q 95 -10 150 110 Q 205 230 260 110 Q 315 -10 370 110 Q 425 230 460 165' fill='none' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2'/><text x='466' y='154' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>y=−½</text><text x='466' y='194' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='700'>y=−1</text><circle cx='277' cy='150' r='4' fill='#16a34a'/><circle cx='353' cy='150' r='4' fill='#16a34a'/><circle cx='315' cy='190' r='5' fill='#16a34a'/><text x='277' y='208' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>210°</text><text x='315' y='208' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>270°</text><text x='353' y='208' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle' fill='#16a34a'>330°</text></svg>

steps: **Add the three solutions:** $$210° + 270° + 330° = 810°$$

questions.promptTex: \text{For the function } y = -3\sin\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}x + \pi\right) + 4, \\ \text{which pairing of amplitude and period is correct?}
hints: For $y = A\sin(Bx + C) + D$: amplitude is $|A|$ — always a positive number.
Period is $\dfrac{2\pi}{|B|}$. Here $B = \tfrac{1}{2}$.
questions.explanation: Amplitude $= |A| = |-3| = 3$. Period $= \dfrac{2\pi}{|B|} = \dfrac{2\pi}{1/2} = 4\pi$.
steps: **Read off $A$ and $B$ from $y = A\sin(Bx + C) + D$.** Here $A = -3$, $B = \tfrac{1}{2}$, $C = \pi$, $D = 4$.
**Amplitude $= |A|$.** Amplitude is a *distance* (midline to peak), so it's always positive: $$|A| = |-3| = 3$$ The negative sign just flips the graph — it doesn't change the amplitude.
**Period $= \dfrac{2\pi}{|B|}$.** Plug in $B = \tfrac{1}{2}$: $$\dfrac{2\pi}{1/2} = 2\pi \cdot 2 = 4\pi$$
**Intuition.** $B < 1$ stretches the graph horizontally, so the period gets *longer* than the standard $2\pi$. A coefficient of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ doubles the period to $4\pi$ ✓.
So the correct pairing is amplitude $= 3$, period $= 4\pi$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the maximum value of the function } y = 6\sin(3x - \tfrac{\pi}{4}) + 1?
hints: Sine values live in $[-1, 1]$. The maximum of $\sin(\text{anything})$ is $1$.
Plug $\sin(\cdots) = 1$ into the equation: $y_\max = 6 \cdot 1 + 1$.
Phase shifts and horizontal stretches don't change the maximum value — only $A$ and $D$ do.
questions.explanation: The maximum of $\sin$ is $1$. So $y_\max = 6(1) + 1 = 7$. (Equivalently: max $=$ midline $+$ amplitude $= 1 + 6 = 7$.)
steps: **Where is the maximum?** $\sin$ peaks at $1$, regardless of what's inside its parentheses. So the *largest* value $\sin(3x - \tfrac{\pi}{4})$ can take is $1$.
**Plug in.** $$y_\max = 6 \cdot 1 + 1 = 7$$
**Shortcut for $y = A\sin(\cdots) + D$:**
$$\boxed{y_\max = D + |A|}, \qquad y_\min = D - |A|$$
Here midline $D = 1$ and amplitude $|A| = 6$, so $y_\max = 1 + 6 = 7$ ✓
**Why the answer isn't $6$ or $5$.** The $6$ is the amplitude (distance from midline to peak), not the peak itself. The peak is the midline ($1$) *plus* the amplitude ($6$).

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the phase shift of } y = 2\sin(3x + \pi) - 1?
hints: For $y = A\sin(Bx + C) + D$, phase shift $= -\dfrac{C}{B}$.
Here $C = +\pi$ and $B = 3$, so phase shift $= -\dfrac{\pi}{3}$.
A **negative** phase shift means the graph moves to the **left**.
questions.explanation: Phase shift $= -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{\pi}{3}$. Negative → shifted $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ units to the left.
steps: **Read $B$ and $C$** from $y = A\sin(Bx + C) + D$. Here $A = 2$, $B = 3$, $C = \pi$, $D = -1$.
**Apply the phase-shift formula.** $$\text{phase shift} = -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{\pi}{3}$$
**Interpret the sign.** A positive phase shift moves the graph **right**; a negative one moves it **left**. So this graph is shifted $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$ units to the **left**.

**Where does this come from?** Rewrite the argument by factoring out $B$: $$3x + \pi = 3\!\left(x + \tfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = 3\!\left(x - \left(-\tfrac{\pi}{3}\right)\right)$$ The form $\sin\!\big(B(x - h)\big)$ has phase shift $h$, here $h = -\tfrac{\pi}{3}$ → shift left by $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{The graph below shows a transformed sine wave with maximum } 5, \\ \text{minimum } -1, \text{ period } \pi, \text{ and passes through its midline going up at } x = 0. \\ \text{Which equation describes it?}
questions.promptSvg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 240' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='130' x2='460' y2='130' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='60' y1='15' x2='60' y2='220' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='20' y1='100' x2='460' y2='100' stroke='#e2e8f0' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><line x1='20' y1='50' x2='460' y2='50' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='1.3' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><line x1='20' y1='190' x2='460' y2='190' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.3' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><path d='M 60 100 Q 95 35 130 100 Q 165 165 200 100 Q 235 35 270 100 Q 305 165 340 100 Q 375 35 410 100 Q 432 132 460 165' fill='none' stroke='#4f46e5' stroke-width='2.4'/><text x='52' y='54' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#16a34a' font-weight='600'>y=5</text><text x='52' y='104' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#64748b'>y=2</text><text x='52' y='194' font-size='11' text-anchor='end' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600'>y=−1</text><line x1='130' y1='126' x2='130' y2='134' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='200' y1='126' x2='200' y2='134' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='270' y1='126' x2='270' y2='134' stroke='#475569' stroke-width='1'/><text x='130' y='148' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>π/2</text><text x='200' y='148' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>π</text><text x='270' y='148' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>3π/2</text></svg>
hints: **Midline** $D = \dfrac{\max + \min}{2} = \dfrac{5 + (-1)}{2}$.
**Amplitude** $|A| = \dfrac{\max - \min}{2} = \dfrac{5 - (-1)}{2}$.
**$B$** from period: $B = \dfrac{2\pi}{\text{period}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\pi}$. The graph crosses midline going up at $x = 0$ → no phase shift.
questions.explanation: Midline $D = 2$, amplitude $A = 3$, $B = 2\pi/\pi = 2$, no phase shift → $y = 3\sin(2x) + 2$.
steps: **Step 1 — Midline ($D$).** Midline is halfway between max and min: $$D = \dfrac{\max + \min}{2} = \dfrac{5 + (-1)}{2} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Amplitude ($A$).** Distance from midline to max (or to min): $$|A| = \dfrac{\max - \min}{2} = \dfrac{5 - (-1)}{2} = 3$$ Graph goes *up* first from the midline at $x = 0$, so use $+A$, not $-A$.
**Step 3 — $B$ from the period.** $$B = \dfrac{2\pi}{\text{period}} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\pi} = 2$$
**Step 4 — Phase shift ($C$).** The graph crosses the midline going *up* exactly at $x = 0$ — that's where $\sin$ naturally starts. So **no phase shift**: $C = 0$.
**Assemble:** $$y = 3\sin(2x) + 2$$
**Verify with one point.** At $x = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$: $\sin(2 \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{4}) = \sin\tfrac{\pi}{2} = 1$, so $y = 3(1) + 2 = 5$ ✓ — that's the maximum, exactly where the graph peaks.

questions.promptTex: \text{Starting from } y = \sin(x), \text{ the graph is transformed as follows:} \\ \text{(1) reflected across the } x\text{-axis}, \quad \text{(2) vertically stretched by a factor of } 4, \\ \text{(3) shifted } \tfrac{\pi}{3} \text{ units to the left}, \quad \text{(4) shifted } 2 \text{ units down.} \\ \text{Which equation represents the new graph?}
hints: **Reflection across the $x$-axis** flips the sign of $A$: $\sin x \to -\sin x$.
**Vertical stretch by 4** multiplies $A$ by $4$: $-\sin x \to -4\sin x$.
**Shift left by $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$** replaces $x$ with $x + \tfrac{\pi}{3}$ (the trick is that *left* shifts go with the *plus* sign).
**Shift down by $2$** subtracts $2$ at the end: $\ldots - 2$.
questions.explanation: Apply each transformation in order: reflect → $-\sin x$; stretch by $4$ → $-4\sin x$; shift left by $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$ → $-4\sin(x + \tfrac{\pi}{3})$; shift down by $2$ → $-4\sin(x + \tfrac{\pi}{3}) - 2$.
steps: **Build the equation one transformation at a time.** Each step modifies the previous formula.
**Start:** $y = \sin(x)$.
**(1) Reflect across the $x$-axis** — multiply the whole function by $-1$. The amplitude coefficient becomes negative. $$y = -\sin(x)$$
**(2) Vertical stretch by factor $4$** — multiply the function (or its amplitude) by $4$. $$y = -4\sin(x)$$

**(3) Shift left by $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$** — replace $x$ with $\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$. *Left* shifts use a **plus** sign inside (it's the opposite of what you might expect — see the why below). $$y = -4\sin\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$$
**(4) Shift down by $2$** — subtract $2$ from the whole function. $$y = -4\sin\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) - 2$$

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='460' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='240' y1='15' x2='240' y2='205' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='20' y1='150' x2='460' y2='150' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><path d='M 60 110 Q 105 80 150 110 Q 195 140 240 110 Q 285 80 330 110 Q 375 140 420 110' fill='none' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.4' stroke-dasharray='3 3'/><text x='340' y='86' font-size='11' fill='#94a3b8'>y = sin x</text><path d='M 60 30 Q 105 230 150 30 Q 195 230 240 30' fill='none' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1' stroke-dasharray='2 2'/><path d='M 30 150 Q 75 -10 120 150 Q 165 310 210 150 Q 255 -10 300 150 Q 345 310 390 150 Q 425 230 460 200' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.2'/><text x='340' y='198' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600'>final: −4 sin(x+π/3) − 2</text><text x='246' y='154' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600'>midline y = −2</text></svg>

steps: **Why left-shift uses $+$ inside.** Think about it this way: the function $\sin(x + \tfrac{\pi}{3})$ already gives the answer that $\sin$ would give at the point $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$ ahead of $x$. So at $x = 0$, you're seeing $\sin(\tfrac{\pi}{3})$ — the value that used to appear $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$ to the right. The graph has been *pulled left* by $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$.
**Final answer:** $y = -4\sin\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) - 2$.

questions.promptTex: \text{What is the phase shift of } y = \sin(2x - \pi) + 3? \\ \textbf{Beware of the classic trap:} \text{ don't read } \pi \text{ directly as the phase shift.}
hints: **Don't read $\pi$ off directly!** The number inside the parentheses is **not** the phase shift on its own — it has to be divided by $B$ first.
Rewrite the argument by **factoring $B$ out**: $2x - \pi = 2\!\left(x - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$.
Now it's in the form $\sin\!\big(B(x - h)\big)$ with $h = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ → shift **right** by $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
questions.explanation: Factor $B = 2$ out: $\sin(2x - \pi) = \sin(2(x - \pi/2))$. Phase shift is $\pi/2$ to the right — not $\pi$.
steps: **The trap.** Many students look at $\sin(2x - \pi)$ and immediately say 'shifted right by $\pi$.' That's **wrong** — the inside number has to be divided by $B$ first.

**The fix: always factor $B$ out before reading off the shift.** Rewrite the argument: $$2x - \pi = 2\!\left(x - \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ Now compare to the cleaner form: $$y = \sin\!\big(B(x - h)\big) + D$$ Here $B = 2$ and $h = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
**The phase shift is $h$.** Positive $h$ → shift **right**. So the graph is shifted $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ units to the **right**.
**Or use the formula:** $$\text{phase shift} = -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{-\pi}{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ (right)}$$

steps.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 480 220' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='460' y2='110' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='100' y1='15' x2='100' y2='205' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.2'/><path d='M 100 110 Q 130 50 160 110 Q 190 170 220 110 Q 250 50 280 110 Q 310 170 340 110 Q 370 50 400 110 Q 430 170 460 110' fill='none' stroke='#94a3b8' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='4 3'/><path d='M 100 110 Q 130 170 160 110 Q 190 50 220 110 Q 250 170 280 110 Q 310 50 340 110 Q 370 170 400 110 Q 430 50 460 110' fill='none' stroke='#dc2626' stroke-width='2.2'/><text x='30' y='148' font-size='11' fill='#94a3b8'>y = sin(2x)</text><text x='30' y='80' font-size='11' fill='#dc2626' font-weight='600'>y = sin(2x − π)</text><path d='M 100 188 L 160 188' stroke='#16a34a' stroke-width='2'/><polygon points='160,188 152,184 152,192' fill='#16a34a'/><text x='130' y='205' font-size='11' fill='#16a34a' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>shift = π/2 right</text></svg>
steps.text: Compare the two curves: $\sin(2x)$ vs $\sin(2x - \pi)$. The red curve is the dashed one shifted right by $\pi/2$ — not by $\pi$.

steps: **Sanity check.** Pick a point. $\sin(2x) = 0$ going up at $x = 0$. $\sin(2x - \pi) = 0$ when $2x - \pi = 0$, i.e., $x = \pi/2$. So the 'starting' point of the wave moved from $0$ to $\pi/2$ — a shift of $\pi/2$ right ✓.

questions.promptTex: \text{Which rewrite of } y = \sin(4x + \pi) \text{ correctly factors out } B \\ \text{so the phase shift can be read off directly?}
hints: Factoring $4$ out of $(4x + \pi)$ means writing it as $4 \cdot (\text{something})$. What's inside? $\dfrac{4x + \pi}{4} = x + \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
So $4x + \pi = 4\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$.
Distribute back to check: $4 \cdot x + 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 4x + \pi$ ✓.
questions.explanation: $4x + \pi = 4(x + \pi/4)$. The $\pi$ has to be divided by $B = 4$ when you factor it out, not just dropped inside.
steps: **The trap.** A common error is to write $4x + \pi = 4(x + \pi)$ — but distributing back gives $4x + 4\pi$, not $4x + \pi$. The constant has to be divided by $B$ when you factor.
**Factor $B = 4$ correctly.** Divide every term inside by $4$: $$4x + \pi = 4\!\left(\dfrac{4x}{4} + \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right) = 4\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
**Verify by distributing back:** $$4 \cdot x + 4 \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 4x + \pi \checkmark$$

**Now the phase shift is obvious.** The factored form is $$y = \sin\!\big(B(x - h)\big) \text{ with } B = 4, h = -\dfrac{\pi}{4}$$ Phase shift is $h = -\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ → shifted $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ to the **left**.

**Why this matters.** Choice (a) $\sin(4(x + \pi))$ would actually mean a shift of $\pi$ to the left, not $\pi/4$. That's a 4× error — exactly the trap. Always factor by *dividing* the constant by $B$.

questions.promptTex: \text{Two students disagree about the phase shift of } y = \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}x - \dfrac{\pi}{4}\right). \\ \text{Anna says: 'phase shift } = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \text{ right.'} \\ \text{Brian says: 'phase shift } = \dfrac{1}{2} \text{ right.'} \\ \text{Who is correct, and why?}
choices: Anna — she read } -C \text{ directly as the phase shift.
Brian — he divided } -C \text{ by } B \text{ as the formula requires.
Both are right; they're equivalent expressions.
Neither — the actual phase shift is } \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ right.
Neither — the phase shift is to the } \textbf{left}, \text{ not the right.
hints: Identify $B$ and $C$ from $y = \sin(Bx + C)$. Here $B = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ and $C = -\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
Phase shift $= -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{-\pi/4}{\pi/2}$. Be careful dividing fractions.
$-\dfrac{-\pi/4}{\pi/2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
questions.explanation: Phase shift $= -\dfrac{C}{B} = \dfrac{\pi/4}{\pi/2} = \dfrac{1}{2}$. Brian is right. Anna fell into the trap of reading $-C$ ($= \pi/4$) without dividing by $B$.
steps: **Identify $B$ and $C$.** Match $y = \sin(Bx + C)$: $$B = \dfrac{\pi}{2}, \quad C = -\dfrac{\pi}{4}$$
**Anna's mistake.** She saw $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ inside and reported the phase shift as $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ to the right. But that's reading $-C$ — without dividing by $B$.
**Brian's correct work.** Use the full formula: $$\text{phase shift} = -\dfrac{C}{B} = -\dfrac{-\pi/4}{\pi/2}$$
Dividing fractions — multiply by the reciprocal: $$= \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot \dfrac{2}{\pi} = \dfrac{2\pi}{4\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Confirm by factoring $B$ out.** $$\dfrac{\pi}{2}x - \dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{2}\!\left(x - \dfrac{1}{2}\right)$$ (Check: $\dfrac{\pi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ ✓.) So the function is $\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}(x - \tfrac{1}{2})\right)$ — shifted $\dfrac{1}{2}$ unit to the **right**.

**Brian is right: shift = $\dfrac{1}{2}$.** The general rule: $$\boxed{\text{always view the equation as } y = a\sin\!\big(b(x - \tfrac{c}{b})\big) + d}$$ The phase shift is $\dfrac{c}{b}$, **not** just $c$.
